behaviors.description: A plugin whose manifest is missing a required field is rejected before any code from that plugin is executed.
behaviors.justification: Failure boundary is between manifest validation and module-loading lifecycle, not just schema-check logic. A pure unit test of `validateManifest()` would miss regressions where loader still imports plugin code after validation failure. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text is limited to local loader behavior (`manifest.json`, reject, no plugin code execution) and does not require subprocesses, network, or external APIs.
behaviors.test_description: Create a temp plugin directory with an invalid `manifest.json` (missing required field) and an entry module that writes a sentinel (e.g., file/global flag) on import. Run real loader against this directory and assert: (1) plugin is reported rejected for manifest error, (2) sentinel was never written, proving no plugin code executed.

behaviors.description: When plugin A declares a dependency on plugin B, plugin B's `onLoad` hook is called before plugin A's.
behaviors.justification: This is a cross-module orchestration failure mode: dependency resolution output must drive hook invocation order. Unit-testing only topo-sort or only hook runner can miss real handoff bugs. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text only concerns local dependency ordering and hook call sequence; no real OS/network/subprocess or AI dependency is required.
behaviors.test_description: Set up two test plugins in a temp directory: A depends on B. Each `onLoad` appends its name to a shared in-process log/file. Run loader and assert log order is `[B, A]`.

behaviors.description: When two plugins each declare a dependency on the other, the loader reports the cycle and loads neither.
behaviors.justification: Cycle bugs appear at dependency-graph + load-execution boundary: detection must prevent lifecycle execution. A unit test of cycle detection alone may not catch loader still attempting loads. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text is purely local graph/orchestration logic and explicitly about reporting + not loading, with no external infrastructure or model output dependency.
behaviors.test_description: Create plugins A and B with mutual dependencies. Give each entry/hook a side effect marker. Run loader and assert: (1) cycle error/report includes A<->B, (2) neither plugin entry nor `onLoad` side effects occurred.

behaviors.description: A plugin's `onLoad` hook is only invoked after the plugin's entry module has been imported and executed.
behaviors.justification: This behavior is about sequencing across two loader stages (module import/execute then hook invocation). Unit tests of either stage alone can miss ordering regressions. System was considered and rejected because behavior text does not require subprocess/OS-level effects beyond normal in-process module loading; Agentic/Workflow are disqualified since no AI steps exist.
behaviors.test_description: Create a plugin whose entry module sets `initialized=true` as import side effect; its `onLoad` asserts that flag is already true and records success. Run loader and assert `onLoad` passed and no ordering assertion failed.

behaviors.description: When a plugin throws an error during its `onLoad` hook, the loader continues initializing the remaining plugins that do not depend on it.
behaviors.justification: Failure is in error-propagation and dependency-aware continuation across loader modules (hook executor + dependency graph + scheduler). Unit tests of isolated try/catch or graph logic can miss real continuation behavior. Higher levels were rejected because behavior text scopes to local initialization flow; no subprocess/network/external API/LLM dependence is present.
behaviors.test_description: Create plugins: X (throws in `onLoad`), Y (independent), Z (depends on X), W (independent). Run loader and assert: X failed, Y and W `onLoad` executed, Z did not execute due to dependency on failed X.
